--- title: "Forecasting 2.0" description: "Whats happening in the space and how to chose the correct model?" author: "Malte Nalenz" date: "2026-05-20" categories: [template, examples] image: thumbnail.png image-alt: "Example thumbnail for the post" draft: true format: html: code-fold: false code-tools: false fig-cap-location: bottom execute: echo: false warning: false message: false --- A short lead paragraph that summarizes the post in two or three sentences. This sits above the first heading and shows up as the listing preview when the `description` field is empty. ## A simple diagram Mermaid diagrams render natively in Quarto — no extra setup needed. ```{mermaid} --- config: theme: neutral layout: elk --- flowchart TD A{large
number TS?} -->|yes| B{Share similar
pattern?} A -->|no| C{Interpretability
required?} C -->|no| D[Pretrained Transformer] C -->|yes| E[Classic Time Series] B -->|no| D B -->|yes| F[Global ML forecasting] ``` Use Mermaid for pipelines, sequence diagrams, decision trees, or anything otherwise diagrammable. Full syntax reference: [mermaid-js.github.io](https://mermaid-js.github.io/). ## A Python plot Code cells execute at render time. The first run can be slow; subsequent renders use Quarto's freeze cache (`_freeze/`), so unchanged posts are not re-executed in CI. ::: {#cell-fig-forecast .cell fig-height='4' fig-width='8' execution_count=1} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Synthetic demand series with a simple rolling-mean forecast.](index_files/figure-html/fig-forecast-output-1.png){#fig-forecast width=660 height=470} ::: ::: The figure above is referenced as [@fig-forecast] via its `label`. Cross-references work for figures, tables, equations, and sections. ## Prose with math and emphasis Inline math: $\hat{y}_{t+1} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} y_{t-i}$. Display math: $$ \text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left| \frac{y_t - \hat{y}_t}{y_t} \right| $$ {#eq-mape} Refer to equations with [@eq-mape]. ## Callouts for asides ::: {.callout-note} Use callouts to highlight context, caveats, or side-notes without breaking the flow of the main argument. ::: ::: {.callout-warning} ## When rolling means break A rolling-mean baseline is only honest on stationary series. If trend or regime shifts dominate, it under-reacts and the bias compounds. ::: ## Tables | Method | MAPE | Notes | |------------------------|-----:|----------------------------------------| | Naive (last value) | 12.4 | Strong baseline; surprisingly hard to beat. | | Rolling mean (14d) | 8.1 | Smooths weekly seasonality. | | Seasonal naive (7d) | 6.7 | Captures the dominant weekly cycle. | | LightGBM with lags | 4.9 | Best with feature engineering. | : Forecast accuracy on the synthetic series above. {#tbl-results} See [@tbl-results] for the comparison. ## Wrapping up Close with the takeaway: one or two sentences on what the reader should remember. Link to the next post or a related project if useful.
